speaker: interviewer
text: February 16, 1996. The survivor is Raymonde Fiol. And this is Tape 2. Could you tell me more about the house that you lived in and the cafe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm, OK. I remember the cafe, obviously, was downstairs-- big front room. Behind it was a, a kitchen, very big kitchen, and private quarters where most of the cooking was done for, obviously, the cafe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cooked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they all did-- Mr. Cailac, Madame Cailac, and the daughter. They all-- it was a big family effort. And I think I, I helped. I was-- I remember going to the garden and picking vegetables and helping, just helping out. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the garden, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just picking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just picking, yes. Upstairs, I believe, were three bedrooms and a bathroom. And I had my own room. And it was a nice house, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they seemed frightened of housing you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I think there was the uneasiness every time the-- every time the German commandant would come in, absolutely. There was always that fear that they may decide to take me, take them at the same time for harboring me. And there was also the fear of the daughter being caught because she was working with the French Free Forces. Yes, there definitely was. There's no question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember conversations that they had with her about her work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they tried to keep it as quiet as possible. It was hushed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of actions, but very few words so that they shouldn't be overheard, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a person was the daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. She was very sweet to me. They all took me under their wings. They were very kind to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were what religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Protestant, I know that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of school was it that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to regular public school. But I also had to go on Sundays and, I believe, maybe other days-- Wednesday is religious education day-- and I had to go then in the church and get the catechism lessons and do homework, and whatnot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was Catholic church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family go on Sundays?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they sent me. I don't believe they went. But I had to go back and trek. It was in town. So I had to go walk and go and attend and come back. Yes, they sent me to church. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any discussions about your being Jewish with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was never mentioned. I think they tried to assimilate me and probably hoping I would stay with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear at any point from any member of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until after the war, no. No. I did-- I kept on asking when would my parents come back. And they said, after the war, when it's over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever talk about Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my knowledge. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did they serve in the restaurant in your recollection?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- it must have been plain, like-- more like a trucker's stop type of food. I know they had wine. [LAUGHS] Even in the war, I guess the French will have wine. And because they were able to grow a lot of their own vegetables in the-- I guess they probably all tended their little farm they had. It wasn't really a farm. It was just vegetable gardens. And I think they did, come to think of it-- they used to make preserves for the winter and, I guess, tap on that in-- to serve. And maybe they had access to other foods, too, being a restaurant, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yes and, and no. I was-- I was in a situation where I felt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not home. I want to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was home at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being with my parents, wherever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- just being with them. It was OK, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to the family about any other members of your original family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just my parents, yeah. Well, I-- we had been really isolated from the rest of the family, so I didn't have any contact with the remainders, you know, the other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever ask them how long the war was going to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I might have. I don't recall. I don't recall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To a child, time is, I guess it's different. Your perspective is a little different than as an adult. And I just wanted to know when, when I would go home, yeah. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What other activities were you involved in in the town? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just school. And I'd go back. [LAUGHS] I was-- I used to spend my day walking. [LAUGHS] Coming back, rain or shine, I had to go. There was no question.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a one-room school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the others-- children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely, very, very vaguely. I remember being put in front of the class and-- don't forget, I guess I was a, a city-born child. And maybe I was with other villagers, so I don't-- no, it was just a one-- I remember getting sick and having yellow jaundice. And I was-- and the teacher came to the house to coach me. I remember that. Yeah, I was laid up for several weeks. I remember that, yeah-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] boiled potatoes for weeks on. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That was when you were sick or because of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, well, maybe both. But I remember I wasn't allowed to eat anything else. Probably both. I don't remember eating much meat, come to think of it. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was just busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was the school conducted in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In French, yes. There was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any discussions at school of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I think the French were very stoic about it. I think they're going to carry on as if nothing happened. And no, there was no mention. Don't forget, this was occupied France. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans were just all around and patrolling the village and everything. So they had a foothold in everywhere. And there was no upfront talk. You, you lived your life as if nothing happened, if that was possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything ever happen to the daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: She continued her work with the Underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: She continued her work right through. I-- the reason that I think I found out and they had to tell me is because I kept on asking, why does she disappear at night? And then she would get dressed. And why, why would she, you know, go away? And they'd try to explain it to me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how that made you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared, yes. I'm sure everybody was scared. Parents were scared. And she was, too. But this was their commitment to helping the best they could with the war-- and by saving me from the Germans, from being deported, which ultimately, that was true. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what changed when you were living with them? You mentioned before you started seeing Germans retreating. Were there any changes leading up to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started just-- I guess it was near the end when the, the Americans were making their movement into France. And we were-- the Ardennes is not far from Germany. So they were-- we were-- the house itself was on a main road. And equipment, heavy equipment, men, seven days and seven nights, constant, just going-- they didn't stop. At first, we were extremely frightened because we didn't know what they would do to us. But their concern was more into retreating and just getting back into Germany as fast as they could. And after seven days and seven nights, then the Americans appeared right behind them with the equipment. And I guess this is when we realized we were freed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember someone telling you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I remember the jubilance of everybody around, and the cheering, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it more.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's just-- don't forget, it was only two houses. So-- but, you know, our elation of being told it's the end of the war. And that's when I asked for my parents. [CRYING] But they didn't come. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of year was this, would you guess?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had to have been [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wintertime. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It may have been, like, around-- I remember the snow because the Germans set up a camp just outside the village. And we would go there. And they had the POWs, the German prisoners, the ones that they had caught before they could leave into-- you know, retreat into Germany. And I remember the snow. Because I remember prisoners being marched naked in the snow by the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were German prisoners--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that the Americans had caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I remember the snow. So it was around-- had to have been-- it was definitely wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they being marched?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I was on the outside of the barbed wires. But I saw them. I was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did that go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess for several months because then I was taken from the Cailacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was probably winter of '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, probably winter of-- the-- it's very hard [SIGHS] to remember. Could be '44. No, it had to have been '45. It had to have been the winter of '45, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your next recollection?

speaker: interviewee
text: My next recollection, I guess we went into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] summer and being told that we have new visitors by Mr. and Mrs. Cailac. They told me we were going to have company. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. I didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know it had anything to do with me. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three people came, a woman-- a young woman-- and a man and a woman, older. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I vaguely remembered the man and the woman-- turned out that was my uncle, my Uncle Gabriel and my Aunt Paula, his wife. And the young lady was my cousin, Stella, from London. She is my father's brother's daughter. And because I gave my name, they were able to trace me. Her brother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was in the British army. And I'm not sure, there were already organization-- Jewish organizations already being formed to try and trace all the survivors. And they were able to locate me. So she was the first civilian to leave England on a boat. And she made her way to find me. And my aunt and uncle were in Italy. See, they made it out of occupied France. And the Germans saved them. Well, actually, the-- I shouldn't say the Germans. The Italians gave them false identity. And they survived in Italy. And then when they came back to France, they, too, were looking for me and looking for my parents as well. Anyway, they all made it back. And I remember they were talking Yiddish. And I said, that sounds very familiar. Although I could not understand, I said, you know, it's, it's mamaloshen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember your cousin Stella?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never knew her. I didn't know her. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your uncle, you had a slight--

speaker: interviewee
text: A slight, yes, recollection. It must have been many years that I hadn't seen him. And then there were obviously discussions, not that I can recall of-- and then they left. And when they left, I remember discussions between the Cailacs and themselves. And then I was told some time later that they were taking me to a place where I would be with children and stuff. It turns out that I didn't want to leave there-- them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Cailacs. I was still waiting for my parents. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask your aunt and uncle about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember asking them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They didn't know. And legally, they couldn't take me because my parents had left them-- had left me with the Cailacs. So it was decided that I was be-- put in an orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunt and uncle weren't allowed to take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So legally, I had to go in an orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, '45, maybe '46-- I was probably going on 9, 10. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they had to leave me there. I think I spent there maybe a year or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The orphanage was not too far from Paris. It's called Andresy. And it was an old castle that they had converted. There were many children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember traveling there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not really. I do not remember traveling there. I remember being left there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who told you you were going to go to an orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Cailacs told me. And they took me there. I don't recall getting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was my first exposure to being with many children, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not happy, didn't know where I was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember my aunt and uncle coming to visit. And I remember people [CRYING] knowing me. But I didn't know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: People in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where they were from. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think these people had been in the labor camp with my parents and had survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told me at that time that when the Germans were retreating and when they thought, you know, was going to be near the end, the French forces helped a lot of the Jews in the labor camp and took them into hiding in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father locked the house and stayed in the house-- locked the door. So what do you think the Germans did? They took them and, you know, deported them. Who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who came [CRYING] to the orphanage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they recounted the whole-- what happened. They were the only ones that had been killed in the whole village-- my parents were. The rest of the village survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My father made many bad moves, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah. So he chose not to go with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the French forces.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Don't ask me why, but they related that to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were the only ones--

speaker: interviewee
text: That were taken from that village. The whole village survived. Don't forget, it was already 1944. They survived through practically the whole war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were told this while you were in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cried-- [SOBBING] the anger, [SOBBING] stupidity. But people make decisions. They think it's for the best, [CRYING] yeah. [CRYING] So I was alone. [CRYING] And during my stay in the orphanage, we had a trip to England. I recall that. And because I had my cousins and my uncle still living-- that's the father of the cousin that had to come-- to see me in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stella's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my Uncle Leon. I was allowed to stay with them, and I met them. So I think I stayed a couple weeks in London. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Great-- [SOBBING] having family. [SOBBING] My cousin at the time was not married. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soon after, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was taken to my aunt and uncle in Paris. That's my Uncle Gabriel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soon after you traveled to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went back to the orphanage. And then I remember being put on a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall how I got there. Maybe they came and get-- to get me. And I stayed some time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I'm past 10 by then. And I was taken to my aunt and uncle and was told I was going to live with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, at that point, I wanted to go to England. [LAUGHS] But I found out later that there was, like-- a family council was formed of the uncle in England and the uncle in-- Gabriel in Paris-- he had no children-- and remaining cousins and aunts that were-- that had survived the war. And it was decided that they would be my guardians. There was a whole formality because I was, really, a ward of the state. So the state declared my aunt and uncle my guardians. And I was to live with them. So I was taken out of the orphanage and brought to them to live in Paris. And at the time, it was my-- bless you--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was my parent's apartment because my aunt and uncle had everything taken from them. Their lives were spared, but nothing left. The Germans had-- he, he was a bookbinder. And they had taken all his equipment and ransacked the apartment. It was no longer free. So, for awhile, we lived in my, my own apartment-- in my parent's apartment. And then we ultimately moved into another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like going back to the apartment for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was mixed emotions. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a familiar to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. Yes, it was. We didn't stay long. We didn't stay very long there. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they talking to you about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you asking questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, after all the moving around, I really wanted to go back to the Cailacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in communication with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't allowed because at that point, I think they had been fighting to keep me. And at that point, I wanted to go back with them. So I was having a lot of nervous tantrums. I remember throwing a lot of tantrums.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having done that when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a child when you were with the Cailacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually carefree. They were very kind. They were very loving. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a sense, I felt, even though we were the occupied France and the Germans were around, I felt somewhat protected by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you felt that way with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt more fear with my parents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just probably their own fear being reflected on me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt safer with the Cailacs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhat.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that period.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go back one more time to the time with the Cailacs, when you saw the German prisoners being marched inside the POW camp, how did you know what you were seeing? How did you know who the prisoners were? Do you remember being told?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes. I remember Evelyn took me-- the Cailacs' daughter, Evelyn Cailac. She took me. And she said, come, we'll go see. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- I, I felt very happy because at that point, I had, I guess, feelings that something had happened to my parents. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was happy too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember her telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Absolutely, yeah. Don't forget, you're, you're-- these were people who really had made a very serious effort to fight the war in their own way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So yes, there was definitely elation. And I felt my own, too. I remember that, yeah.
